title: GPT's Causal Language Modeling
concept: GPT is trained by predicting the next word, only looking at past words, matching natural left-to-right reading.

exercises.prompt: When GPT writes a story, what does it try to predict at each step?

options.text: The first word of the next chapter

exercises.explain: GPT works by predicting the very next word in a sequence, repeating this process to build entire paragraphs.

cards.title: One Word at a Time
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how GPT models write so smoothly? They don't magically write whole paragraphs at once. They build sentences one single word at a time, just like we speak!

cards.title: Causal Language Modeling
cards.body: Causal Language Modeling is a training method where an AI predicts the next word in a sequence. Crucially, the AI is only allowed to look at past words (to the left) and is blocked from seeing future words (to the right).

cards.title: The Cardboard Slider
cards.body: Imagine you are reading a exciting adventure book. To avoid spoilers, you cover the upcoming page with a cardboard sheet. You read left-to-right, trying to guess the very next word based only on the story you have already read. This is exactly how GPT reads and learns!

cards.title: Predicting Step-by-Step
cards.body: Given the partial sentence: "The cat sat on the...", a Causal Language Model hides all words after "the". Using only the past words, it predicts "mat", "rug", or "couch". Once it guesses, it moves one step right and tries again.

cards.title: No Cheating Allowed!
cards.body: Many people think GPT models see the entire completed sentence during their training process. In reality, during training, future words are strictly masked (hidden) so the AI cannot cheat by looking ahead.

cards.title: Causal vs. Bidirectional
cards.body: See how Causal models differ from models that look at both directions at the same time.

left.text: Looks only at the left (past words) to predict what comes next.

right.text: Looks at both left and right (past and future) words at the same time.

exercises.prompt: What is the main task of a Causal Language Model like GPT?

options.text: Predicting the next word using only past words
Translating text into five languages at once
Looking at the last word and guessing the first word

exercises.explain: GPT is a causal language model, meaning it predicts the next word using only the preceding words, moving forward step-by-step.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A Causal Language Model is allowed to look at future words to help predict the next word during its training.
exercises.explain: Causal models are strictly prohibited from looking at future words; otherwise, they wouldn't learn how to generate new text word-by-word.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the reading direction of GPT.
exercises.sentence: GPT reads and predicts text from [blank] to right.
exercises.explain: Causal models mimic human reading by processing text from left to right, using only past words to predict what comes next.

exercises.prompt: Two AI systems are explaining how GPT is trained. Which one is correct?

options.text: AI #1: "GPT uses a mask to hide future words, so it only learns from what came before." Sundar AI.
AI #2: "GPT looks at both the start and end of a sentence at once to fill in the middle." Alt AI.
AI #3: "GPT ignores the order of words entirely and treats them as a random bag." Byte AI.

exercises.explain: AI #1 is correct because Causal Language Models like GPT strictly mask future words to learn how to predict them organically. AI #2 describes bidirectional models (like BERT), not causal ones.

exercises.prompt: When a GPT model is learning to predict the next word in a sentence, how does it handle the words that come after it?

options.text: It reads the entire sentence backwards from right to left first
It only looks at past words on the left, keeping future words hidden
It randomly guesses words from a dictionary without any context
It looks at both the past and future words at the exact same time

exercises.explain: GPT is designed to predict the next word using only the words that came before it. Looking at the future words (to the right) during training would be like cheating on a test by looking at the answer sheet!
